coredata.dc:description: Co-single atom catalysts (Co-SACs) exhibit high selectivity and efficiency for H2O2 electrosynthesis via the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e−ORR), with the catalytic activity as significantly influenced by the loading content of Co atoms. However, the loading of Co atoms is typically confined to a low level (<1.0 wt%), which restricts their overall catalytic performance. Herein, we proposed a defect engineering strategy that utilized the carbon dots (CDs) as a platform to capture Co atoms, and constructed a series of Co-SACs with high Co atoms loading (with a maximum of 6.16 wt%), representing a substantial improvement compared to existing benchmarks in the literature. Experimental and characterization results revealed that the defect sites on CDs allowed adequate spacing between the Co atoms, effectively preventing their aggregation and promoting the generation of highly loaded metal atoms. It is worth noting that ECDs-CoSA demonstrated remarkable efficacy and catalytic activity with 93.70 % H2O2 selectivity and an impressive H2O2 yield of 9.71 mol L−1 h−1 gcat. −1 in neutral condition. This study presents an effective route for the controlled prepared of Co-SACs with high metal loading, aiming at sustainable and the efficient H2O2 electrosynthesis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Co-single atom catalysts (Co-SACs) exhibit high selectivity and efficiency for H2O2 electrosynthesis via the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e−ORR), with the catalytic activity as significantly influenced by the loading content of Co atoms. However, the loading of Co atoms is typically confined to a low level (<1.0 wt%), which restricts their overall catalytic performance. Herein, we proposed a defect engineering strategy that utilized the carbon dots (CDs) as a platform to capture Co atoms, and constructed a series of Co-SACs with high Co atoms loading (with a maximum of 6.16 wt%), representing a substantial improvement compared to existing benchmarks in the literature. Experimental and characterization results revealed that the defect sites on CDs allowed adequate spacing between the Co atoms, effectively preventing their aggregation and promoting the generation of highly loaded metal atoms. It is worth noting that ECDs-CoSA demonstrated remarkable efficacy and catalytic activity with 93.70 % H2O2 selectivity and an impressive H2O2 yield of 9.71 mol L−1 h−1 gcat. −1 in neutral condition. This study presents an effective route for the controlled prepared of Co-SACs with high metal loading, aiming at sustainable and the efficient H2O2 electrosynthesis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e−ORR) is widely recognized as a promising method for H2O2 production, due to its high efficiency and environmental friendliness [1–3]. The electrocatalyst plays a crucial role in 2e−ORR, ultimately determining the selectivity, efficiency and stability of the entire process [3,4]. However, taking the carbon-based Co-single atom catalysts (Co-SACs) that demonstrate high selectivity in the 2e−ORR process as an example, a typical synthesis process for carbon-based Co-SACs start with a bulk carbon support, and utilize their surface functional groups or carbon vacancies and the like to capture the metal precursor [5–7]. Subsequently, a high-temperature pyrolysis process facilitates the formation of metal atoms embedded in the carbon matrix [8,9]. Nevertheless, the absence of effective carrier confinement may lead to accidental agglomeration of Co atoms into clusters or nanoparticles during the annealing process, resulting in a significant reduction in metal loading (<1.0 wt%) [10–13], thereby significantly constraining their areal density of active sites and overall catalytic activity. Consequently, the development of an effective synthesis strategy for increasing the Co atoms loading in Co-SACs is pivotal in this field, which remains as an open challenge.

Notably, as a form of carbon material, zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) are characterized by significant features such as size-limiting effect, abundant functional groups and defect sites [14–16]. Furthermore, the effective dispersion of CDs in solution promotes their anchoring in complete contact with isolated metal atoms, thereby leading to increased metal loading [17,18]. Compared to larger bulk carbon carriers, the size limitation of small CDs can effectively prevent aggregation between metal atoms and improve their atomic dispersion [19,20]. Meanwhile, the dispersion and loading of metal atoms can be further optimized by utilizing a sufficient number of defect sites on the CDs for anchoring the metal atoms [21]. These unique properties render CDs highly promising as an ideal platform for the preparation of high metal loading Co-SACs. Several studies on single atom-CDs catalysts have been reported so far [19,22]. For example, a recent study demonstrated that Pb-SACs loaded on S-doped CDs with S/O super-coordinated structures could achieve highly selective 2e−ORR under alkaline conditions [22]. Nevertheless, the instability of H2O2 in alkaline solutions leads to self-decomposition and increases the difficulty of storage. In contrast, H2O2 is much more stable under neutral condition, and the neutral H2O2 solution is considered as the most flexible form for practical applications [23]. Therefore, designing single atom-CDs catalysts with high metal loading to achieve the target of neutral 2e−ORR remains a promising yet formidable challenge.

With this hypothesis, Co-SACs with high metal loading have been well designed and synthesized by a defect engineering strategy in this study. Specifically, as depicted in Scheme 1 , ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) precursor was employed to synthesis defect-rich carbon dots (ECDs) by small molecule polymerization. Subsequently, Co atoms were trapped on defect sites of ECDs, thereby preventing inter-atomic aggregation and generation of a high Co atoms loading. As a result, ECDs-CoSA with a metal loading of up to 6.16 wt% was successfully synthesized on ECDs. Significantly, the experimental results demonstrated that the 2e−ORR activity and selectivity of the ECDs-CoSA catalysts were enhanced with an increase in Co atoms loading, resulting in a substantial enhancement in H2O2 generation, ultimately achieving a 93.70 % H2O2 selectivity and a 9.71 mol L−1 h−1 gcat. −1H2O2 yield rate. This work focuses on the design of high metal loading Co-SACs to advance the development of H2O2 electrosynthesis.

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, ≥99.0 %), sodium sulphate anhydrous (Na2SO4, ≥99.0 %), cobalt chloride (CoCl2·6H2O), Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95 %), Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %) and Nitric acid (HNO3, 68 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. Titanium oxalate potassium (K2Ti(C2O4)3) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Nafion solution (5 wt%) was obtained from Alfa Aesar. The Nafion 117 membrane was purchased from DuPont. All reagents were used as received and without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study.

The High resolution-transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images and element mapping were observed on a Thermofisher FEI Talos F200XG2. Aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2010 LaB6 high-resolution TEM and double Cs-corrected JEOL JEM-ARM200CF STEM, which were operated at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku Miniflex-600 diffractometer operated at 40 kV voltage and 15 mA current using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) with a step size of 8° min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected on ESCALAB 250Xi and the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV as an internal standard. Raman spectra were taken using a Raman spectroscopy instrument (LabRAM HR Evolution, Horiba) equipped with a green laser emitting at 532 nm. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was recorded on a JES-FA 200 (JEOL). The ICP measurement was performed on Optima 7300 DV inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES). Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) was carried out on UV3600 Shimadzu equipment. In order to determine the bandgaps of the samples, the obtained data were subjected to transformation using the Tauc equation:
( α h ν )
n
= K
( h ν -
E g
)
where α is absorption coefficient, h
ν
is light energy, n is 2 for direct band gap materials, 0.5 for indirect band gap materials, K is constant, Eg is band gap energy.

X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy measurements were performed at the Co K-edge. Data were collected at the BL14W1 beamline of the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility (SSRF), China. The storage ring was operated at 3.5 GeV. Co foil and CoO were used as references. The Co K-edge X-ray absorption near-edge spectroscopy (XANES) data were recorded in fluorescence mode. The hard X-ray was monochromatized with Si (111) double-crystals. The acquired EXAFS data were processed following standard procedures using the ATHENA module of the IFEFFIT software packages, which were obtained by subtracting the post-edge background from the overall absorption and normalizing with respect to the edge-jump step. After that, χ(k) data in the k-space were Fourier transformed to real (R) space using a Hanning window (dk = 1.0 Å−1) to separate the EXAFS contributions from different coordination shells.

A 60 mL aqueous solution of EDTA (0.1 g mL−1) was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a volume of 100 mL and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). Finally, the filtrate was dialyzed with DI water (MW 500) for 48 h to obtain purified ECDs solution.

In a typical synthesis of ECDs-CoSA catalyst, a 10 mg mL−1 Co stock solution was first prepared by dissolving CoCl2·6H2O in DI water. Subsequently, 10 mL of the CoCl2·6H2O solution was dispersed in 50 mL of purified ECDs solution and sonicated for 15 min to obtain a mixed solution. The mixture was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 120 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the ECDs/Co powder was obtained by freeze-drying for 48 h. The dried powder was subjected to high temperature pyrolysis (heating rate, 5 °C min−1) at 400 °C (Ar atmosphere) for 120 min to obtain ECDs-CoSA catalyst. Similarly, ECDs-CoSA0.1 and ECDs-CoSA0.5 were synthesized with the same method except for the cobalt stock solution concentration (1 and 5 mg mL−1, respectively).

HCl and HNO3 were homogeneously mixed according to the volume ratio of 3:1 and configured as aqua regia solution. Subsequently, 20 mg of ECDs-CoSAx catalyst was dispersed in 10 mL of aqua regia solution and stirred continuously at 60 °C for 24 h. After that, 1 mL of the mixture was pipetted and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filters. The obtained solution was diluted 5 times with DI water and then tested for the concentration of each metal element by ICP-OES. The loading of Co in each catalyst was calculated using the following equation:
c
m e t a l
w t %
=
M × a
m V
× 1000
× 100 %
where, cmetal is the mass percentage of metal in the catalyst, M (mg L−1) is the metal content measured in ICP-OES, a is the solution coefficient multiplier, m (mg) is the mass of catalyst dissolved in aqua regia, and V (mL) is the volume of aqua regia solution.

The oxygen reduction reaction performance of all catalysts was evaluated using a standard three-electrode system, with data acquisition and analysis conducted with a CHI 760e electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). A rotating ring disc electrode (RRDE) was employed as the working electrode (PINE Research Instruments, USA), comprising a glassy carbon electrode (disk area: 0.247 cm2) and a Pt ring electrode (ring area: 0.186 cm2). An Ag/AgCl electrode (saturated KCl solution) and a graphite rod were served as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. All recorded potentials were normalized to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to the equation ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 + 0.059 × pH, where the pH of 0.1 M Na2SO4 is maintained at 7.0.

Each catalyst (5 mg) was dispersed into 1 ml solution composed of 4.5:4.5:1 = ethanol:DI water:5 wt% Nafion solution), followed by ultrasonication for 30 min. Subsequently, 10 μL catalyst ink was dropped onto the RRDE and allowed to air-dry (0.2 mg cm−2). All electrochemical measurements were conducted within O2 or N2-saturated 0.1 M Na2SO4. Prior to the onset of 2e−ORR measurements, the RRDE underwent a pre-activation routine involving the CV scans from 0 to 1.0 V vs RHE at a rate of 50 mV s−1 until a stable state was achieved. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were performed at a scan rate of 10 mV/s at a rotational speed of 1600 rpm. During the LSV measurements, the Pt ring potential was held at 1.2 V vs RHE. Specially, the Pt ring was electrochemically purified by conducting CV with the same potential range at a scan rate of 100 mV/s for 20 cycles to eliminate any accumulated PtOx.

The selectivity of H2O2 and the electron transfer number (n) of the samples on the RRDE were determined by calculating the current at both disc and ring electrodes using the following equation:

where Id (A) represent the disk current, Ir (A) is the ring current, and N is the collection efficiency, which is determined as 0.366 [24].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured at 0.3 V vs RHE in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 105 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of catalysts were investigated by analyzing CV curves recorded at various sweep rates (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV/s), and the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was calculated as the following equation:
E C S A =
R f
× S

C

j c
v
where S represents the disk area of the electrode (S = 0.247 cm2), Rf is the roughness factor of the working electrode, jc is the current density of the double-layer capacitor, and v is the scan rate.

In a typical process, 50 mg catalyst was dispersed into 10 mL solution composed of 4.5:4.5:1 = ethanol:DI water:5 wt% Nafion solution). The mixed solution was subjected to sonication for a minimum of 30 min in order to form a homogeneous ink. Subsequently, the well-dispersed ink was subsequently airbrushed onto a gas diffusion electrode (GDE, YLS-30T, Suzhou Sinero Technology Co., Ltd, China, working area 1.0 cm2) as a cathode with a loading of 1.0 mg cm−2. A IrO2-coated Ti-mesh electrode was utilized as the anode, while the Ag/AgCl electrode served as the reference electrode. The practical H2O2 production was conducted through a flow-cell system comprising an O2 gas chambers and two liquid chambers (anion and cation chambers) separated by a Nafion 117 membrane. The electrosynthesis of H2O2 was conducted at constant potential of 0.1 V vs RHE, and the electrolyte was refreshed every 8 h to ensure stable production of H2O2. During the experiment, a 1.0 M Na2SO4 electrolyte was recycled through a peristaltic pump at a consistent flow rate of 15 mL min−1, while maintaining an O2 gas inlet at 50 mL min−1 flowing through the cathode.

The H2O2 concentration was measured using the color development of K2Ti(C2O4)3 titration method, in which the colorless Ti4+ solution would be reduced to a yellow Ti3+ solution by H2O2 (2Ti4+ + H2O2 = 2Ti3+ + 2H+ + O2). Thus, the consumed concentration of Ti4+ can be photometrically measured at the wavelength of 405 nm by using UV–vis spectroscopy. The standard H2O2 concentration-absorbance curve (cH2O2 = 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 mmol/L) was calibrated by mixing known amount of commercial H2O2 solution (30 wt%) with a mixture solution containing 0.1 M H2SO4 and 5 mM K2Ti(C2O4)3, where A = 0.016 + 0.614·cH2O2 (R2 = 0.999).The sample solution was mixed with the standard Ti4+ solution in a volume ratio of 1:10 to quantify the produced H2O2.

The Faradaic efficiency (FE) was calculated according to the following equation:
H 2

F E
( % )

× n × F × V
M
H 2 O 2

where n is the electron transfer number (2 for oxygen reduction to H2O2), F is the Faraday constant (96,485 C mol−1), V (mL) is the volume of the solution, I (A) is the current and t (s) is time.

The synthesis procedure of ECDs-CoSAx catalyst is shown in Fig. 1 a. Briefly, ECDs-CoSAx catalysts were obtained by the trapping of Co atoms on specific defect sites of ECDs, followed by freeze-drying and pyrolysis under Ar flow. Literature analysis indicated that the most of Co-SACs based on carbon carriers reported have exhibited a Co atoms loading around 1.0 wt% (Fig. 1b and Table S1), while the ECDs-CoSAx synthesized in this study demonstrated a significantly higher Co atoms loading of 1.92 (ECDs-CoSA0.1), 4.85 (ECDs-CoSA0.5) and 6.16 wt% (ECDs-CoSA) (Table S2), respectively. This elevated loading content is expected to greatly enhance the catalytic performance of Co-SACs in 2e−ORR. High resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1c) revealed a monodisperse feature of the ECDs with an average diameter <5 nm and the inset presents a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [15,25]. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the ECDs-CoSAx exhibited a morphology of nanosheets, which can be attributed to the gradual splicing of CDs during pyrolysis [19]. Moreover, no obvious metal nanoparticles were observed, indicating the atomic dispersion of Co atoms. Fig. 1d illustrated the Fourier transform fitting results based on Fig. 1c, and the ECDs exhibited evident defect sites, possibly attributed to the dangling bonds of pyridinic nitrogen [26]. Such a well-isolated and defective ECDs may contribute to prevent severe agglomeration of metal atoms, making it an ideal platform for implanting Co single atoms. High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) was conducted to investigate the dispersion properties of Co species at the atomic level. As shown in Fig. 1e, densely and atomically dispersed Co atoms were distinguished from the carbon matrix without clusters and nanoparticles. The atomic dispersion of Co atoms was further confirmed through the atom-overlapping Gaussian-function fitting mapping and analysis of intensity profile (Fig. 1f). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Figs. 1g and S2) of prepared ECDs exhibit a broad peak around 25°, attributing to the (0 0 2) reflections of graphitic carbon [26,27]. Conversely, following the introduction of the Co precursor, diffraction peaks specific to the CoCl2·6H2O phase (PDF#13-0399) emerged in the ECDs/Co [9], substantiating the trapping of Co species by the defect sites of ECDs. After pyrolysis, there was no diffraction peak of Co-related species in ECDs-CoSA, suggesting the absence of Co-nanoparticles formation, which is consistent with the above results. The elemental spectra obtained through energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) display in Fig. 1h also demonstrate the homogeneous distribution of Co species across the entire architecture.

To elucidate the evolution of the defect structure in ECDs pre- and post-loading Co atoms, a series of spectroscopic characterizations were performed. The Raman spectra in Figs. 2a and S3 exhibited two distinct peaks, specifically the defect-related D band at 1350 cm−1 and the graphitic carbon-related G band at 1600 cm−1 [2,4]. Generally, the ID/IG ratio acts as an indicator of the probability of inherent defects in the material, with higher ID/IG values suggesting a greater presence of defect sites [4]. Compared to ECDs (1.07), the ID/IG values of ECDs-CoSA0.1 (0.94), ECDs-CoSA0.5 (0.90) and ECDs-CoSA (0.79) showed a noticeable decrease, indicating that Co atoms effectively occupy defect sites within the original ECDs, resulting in a reduction of defect content. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of ECDs and ECDs-CoSA are depicted in Fig. 2 b, with ECDs exhibiting a strong peak signal at a magnetic field g = 2.003, implying the presence of high-density defects in the ECDs. [28]. Whereas, there is a substantial decrease in defect density after anchoring the Co atoms (ECDs-CoSA), which aligns with the hypothesis that the strategic approach of defect engineering induced metal atoms anchoring.

The full-survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra in Fig. S4 and Table S3 confirmed the presence of C, N, O and Co elements in the prepared catalysts. As depicted in Figs. 2c and S5a, the high-resolution C1s spectrum of ECDs and ECDs-CoSA can be deconvoluted into graphitic carbon (C–C) at 284.8 eV, defects at 285.5 eV, C–O at 286.4 eV, and O–CO at 288.7 eV [4,29]. ECDs-CoSA exhibited a significant increase in C–C content from 21 % to 63 %, owing to the gradual agglomeration of CDs during high-temperature pyrolysis, splicing into a graphite sheet and enhanced graphitization [30]. Meanwhile, the reduction in carbon defects content due to the integration of Co single atom is consistent with the previously mentioned Raman and EPR findings. The N 1s spectra illustrated in Figs. 2d and S5b exhibited three characteristic peaks at 398.6 eV, 400.2 eV, and 401.2 eV, corresponding to pyridine-N (Npyd), pyrrole-N (Npyr), and graphite-N (G-N), respectively [28]. The evaluation of N species content revealed that the Npyr content in ECDs-CoSA (33 %) was relatively low compared to ECDs (55 %), which may be attributed to the gradual conversion of metastable Npyr to the more structurally stable G-N and Npyd during pyrolysis at high temperatures, resulting in an increase of G-N (18 %) content. Whereas, Npyd is dominant in ECDs-CoSA (37 %), Npyd serves as the anchoring site for Co atoms due to its strong ligand affinity [31]. In addition, a new peak was detected at 399.4 eV, unequivocally attributed to the presence of Co–N bonds [31]. Notably, the high-resolution XPS spectra of ECDs-CoSA0.1 and ECDs-CoSA0.5 revealed similar results, as shown in Fig. S6a and b. It was observed that the defect content in ECDs-CoSA0.1 and ECDs-CoSA0.5 decreased to 10 % and 9 %, respectively, while the G-N content increased to 12 % and 15 %, respectively.

To get insight into the chemical state and coordination structure of the ECDs-CoSA, XPS and X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) were performed. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the Co–N peak attributed at 781.0 eV dominates the ECDs-CoSA catalyst, speculating on the possibility of Co–N coordination. In addition, a signal attributed to Co–O at 783.2 eV was observed, revealing the formation of Co–O coordination [31]. Fig. S7 illustrated the O 1s species of ECDs and ECDs-CoSA, where O 1s can be decomposed into CO (531.6 eV) and C–O (533.0 eV) [15]. It is noteworthy that a peak of Co–O bond located at 530.6 eV appears in ECDs-CoSA, which also implies that there is coordination between Co and O [32]. The XPS analysis of prepared ECDs-CoSA0.1 and ECDs-CoSA0.5 revealed similar results (Fig. S6). Observation of Fig. S6b and c indicates that the Co–N and Co–O bonds of the corresponding metals appear near 399 eV in N 1s and 531 eV in O 1s, suggesting similar coordination environments for the prepared ECDs-CoSA0.1 and ECDs-CoSA0.5 catalysts.

The X-ray absorption near-edge spectroscopy (XANES) spectra presented in Fig. 3b revealed that the energy absorption edge for the ECDs-CoSA showed a positive shift, in comparison with that of the Co foil, and is close to CoO, clarifying that the valence state of Co is between 0 and +2. The WT contour plots of Co foil and CoO standards (Fig. 3c) showed the intensity maxima at 8.0 and 7.5 Å−1, which are most likely associated with Co-Co and Co-O, respectively. In contrast, the WT contour plots of the ECDs-CoSA catalyst showed only one intensity maximum at 5.2 Å−1, which can be assigned to the Co–N bond [33]. Consistent with the results of the WT-EXAFS analysis, the Fourier-transformed EXAFS (FT-EXAFS) spectra in Fig. 3d provide evidence for the absence of Co-Co bonds (2.16 Å) in ECDs-CoSA [34], further verifying the isolated dispersion of Co atoms. Furthermore, the primary peak of ECDs-CoSA is situated at 1.59 Å, indicating a correlation with the Co–N coordination structure observed in reported Co-SACs [33,34]. The coordination configuration of Co atoms in the ECDs-CoSA catalyst can be obtained by EXAFS fitting as shown in Figs. 3e and S8. The best fitting results of the EXAFS data in Table S4 demonstrated that the isolated Co atoms were atomically dispersed on the N and O co-doped carbon matrix to form the coordination structure of Co-N3O2. These results strongly suggested that defect engineering can achieve atomic-level dispersion of Co without the existence of metal-derived crystalline structures.

The 2e−ORR performance of prepared catalysts was evaluated to quantify the performance of the H2O2 production on a rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) at 1600 rpm in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. Firstly, cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were performed at room temperature under O2-saturated and N2-saturated conditions to evaluate the performance of all samples (Fig. S9). Compared to the unconsolidated CV curves in N2-saturated solution, each of the samples showed a distinct cathodic ORR peak in O2-saturated solution, indicating the presence of an ORR reaction [5,9]. Fig. 4 a displays the distinctive 2e−ORR performance for ECDs-CoSAx catalysts with the oxygen reduction current measured on the disk electrode (solid lines) and the H2O2 oxidation current on the Pt ring electrode (dashed lines). The Iring in the upper panel in Fig. 4a and Table S5 was compared to evaluate the selectivity toward H2O2 formation. ECDs-CoSA provides the highest ring current of 0.146 mA at 0 V vs RHE, followed by ECDs-CoSA0.5 (0.098 mA), ECDs-CoSA0.1 (0.032 mA) and ECDs (0.010 mA), the overall trend of selectivity for H2O2 formation is ECDs-CoSA > ECDs-CoSA0.5 > ECDs-CoSA0.1 > ECDs. The corresponding H2O2 selectivity and electron transfer numbers were plotted in Fig. 4b and c as a function of potential. Obviously, an increase in Co atom loading from 1.92 wt% in ECDs-CoSA0.1 to 6.16 wt% in ECDs-CoSA resulted in a greater number of available Co active sites for O2 reduction, leading to higher H2O2 currents, i.e., the Iring value. Ultimately achieving a 93.7 % H2O2 selectivity with an electron transfer number (n) of 2.1. By contrast, the absence of Co atoms in ECDs contributed less than 10 % H2O2 selectivity, suggesting that the increase of Co atoms content in ECDs-CoSA has an excellent positive contribution for 2e−ORR. It is worth noting that the C, N, O content remained relatively consistent across all catalysts even after Co atoms doping (Table S3), ruling out their contribution to the change in H2O2 selectivity.

To further elucidate the correlation between the catalytic activity of 2e−ORR and the Co sites on the ECDs-CoSAx catalysts, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of all the catalysts were measured by the double layer capacitance (Cdl) method in N2-saturated 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte (Fig. S10). As depicted in Fig. 4d, the calculated capacitance values for ECDs-CoSA0.1, ECDs-CoSA0.5 and ECDs-CoSA were found to be 0.30, 0.58 and 0.67 mF cm−2, respectively. Considering that the standard electrodes utilized possess a real surface area of 0.247 cm2, the corresponding ECSA (Fig. 4e) were determined as ECDs-CoSA0.1 (1.85 cm2), ECDs-CoSA0.5 (3.58 cm2) and ECDs-CoSA (4.13 cm2), indicated that the best catalytic activity of ECDs-CoSA among these prepared catalysts [34]. Furthermore, the Tafel slope (Fig. 4f) of ECDs-CoSA was determined to be 83.0 mV dec−1, which is significantly smaller than that of ECDs (102.9 mV dec−1), ECDs-CoSA0.1 (92.8 mV dec−1) and ECDs-CoSA0.5 (86.8 mV dec−1), demonstrating the favorable kinetics of H2O2 generation of ECDs-CoSA [17]. Then, the inherent interfacial resistance of the charge carriers was analysed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As illustrated in Fig. S11, ECDs displays the largest radius, while the radius of the ECDs-CoSAx gradually decrease with the increase of Co atoms loading (ECDs > ECDs-CoSA0.1 > ECDs-CoSA0.5 > ECDs-CoSA). In general, a smaller radius implies a lower interfacial resistance, which is more favourable for charge transfer [35,36]. Hence, ECDs-CoSA with the highest Co atoms loading presents the lowest interfacial resistance. This suggest that the charge transfer efficiency of the catalysts can be improved by increasing the loading of Co atoms, thereby favoring the 2e−ORR process. To further investigate the electronic properties of the prepared catalysts, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was conducted, and the Tauc equation was utilized to calculate the band gap energies. As depicted in Fig. S12, the incorporation of Co atoms into ECDs (1.53 eV) resulted in a reduction of the band gap energies for ECDs-CoSA0.1, ECDs-CoSA0.5 and ECDs-CoSA to 0.91, 1.08 and 0.64 eV, respectively. The narrowing of the band gap energy facilitates the excitation of charge carriers into the conduction band and improves the electrical conductivity of the catalysts [37–39], consistent with the observed increase in EIS results. Moreover, the long-term stability of the ECDs-CoSA catalyst was measured using the chronoamperometry (CA) method with the disc voltage set to a constant 0.1 V vs RHE. As shown in Fig. S13, the selectivity of H2O2 was maintained at more than 90 % during 12 h of continuous testing, which indicates that the ECDs-CoSA catalysts have strong electrocatalytic stability. Based on the above results, it can be demonstrated that the ECDs-CoSA catalysts have great potential for excellent performance in 2e−ORR to H2O2 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical insights into the 2e−ORR catalytic activity of ECDs-CoSA
ce:section.ce:para: Above, we have effectively improved the selectivity and activity towards 2e−ORR by increasing the loading of Co atoms in the ECDs-CoSA catalyst. To further reveal the potential role of the Co sites, the ECDs-CoSA after reaction was characterized and analyzed to investigate possible variations of catalyst’s electronic structure. As depicted in Fig. S14a, the energy positions at 0.8 on Co K-edge XANES spectra for ECDs-CoSA after 2e−ORR exhibited a shift towards lower energy and the valence state of Co was reduced, indicating that Co sits served as the active center for promoting 2e−ORR [35,40]. Notably, XPS analyses yielded consistent findings, showing a slight shift of the Co–N bond from 781.0 to 780.5 eV, thereby confirming the reduction of the Co valence state (Fig. S15) [40]. Moreover, the Fourier-transform k3-weighted EXAFS spectra of ECDs-CoSA after reaction was shown in Fig. S14b, demonstrating the slight elongation of the Co–N bond length from 1.59 Å to 1.62 Å. This observation suggested that the binding of the Co site to oxygen species during the 2e−ORR process may have caused Co–N bond elongation [40,41], thus highlighting the significant role played by Co sites in facilitating the 2e−ORR process.

Then, the Density-functional theory (DFT) calculations were further conducted to elucidate the correlation between the Co sites in ECDs-CoSA and 2e−ORR catalytic activity. Based on the EXAFS results, ECDs-CoSA was identified as an axially coordinated Co-N3O2 structure, with an O atom present on the underlying graphene. Subsequently, three structure models were constructed as Co-N3O2 (I), Co-N3O2 (II) and Co-N3O2 (III), respectively (Fig. 5 a). To accurately assess the catalytic activity of Co-N3O2, the adsorption behavior for O2 was first examined (Fig. 5b). It is important to note that a more negative Eads value indicates a higher adsorption strength of the catalyst for O2 [37]. Obviously, for all Co-N3O2 models, O2 is more likely to be adsorbed in the end-on adsorption configuration, which is generally considered to effectively prevent the cleavage of O–O bonds [23], thereby increasing the selectivity of H2O2 production while maintaining high activity. In addition, all Eads values are negative, indicating that the ECDs-CoSA catalyst can adsorb O2 well and promote the progress of 2e−ORR.

On the other hand, the 2e−ORR catalytic activity of the catalysts was also investigated, and the free energy diagrams are presented in Fig. 5c. The free energy values for the conversion from O2 to *OOH are consistently negative, indicating a reduction in the energy barrier for *OOH formation that is thermodynamically favorable [23,33]. Additionally, all models of Co-N3O2 exhibit exothermic behavior during the reduction of *OOH to H2O2, and more negative free energy values are conducive to promoting this process, suggesting that the adsorption of *OOH on Co-N3O2 is more favorable for the subsequent hydrogenation to produce H2O2 [42]. Throughout the entire reaction process, UL serves as a crucial indicator for evaluating the catalytic activity of Co-N3O2, with lower UL values corresponding to heightened 2e−ORR catalytic activity [23]. Clearly, all Co-N3O2 models consistently exhibit excellent 2e−ORR catalytic activity, with Co-N3O2 (III) exhibiting an UL value of only 0.10 V, once again indicating the remarkable selectivity of ECDs-CoSA on the 2e−ORR. The adsorption strength of *OOH species is considered as a pivotal descriptor in determining the 2e−ORR selectivity of catalysts [22,36]. Therefore, in order to further study the effect of *OOH on Co-N3O2 (I), Co-N3O2 (II), Co-N3O2 (III) for 2e−ORR catalytic activity, the density of states (DOS) of *OOH is calculated. As shown in Fig. 5d, near the Fermi level, the orbits of Co 3d and O 2p overlap significantly, which indicates that there is an obvious orbital hybridization between them [22,37]. This evidence demonstrates that *OOH can interact with Co-N3O2 (I), Co-N3O2 (II) and Co-N3O2 (III), providing a robust foundation for the catalyst in the 2e−ORR process.

Taking inspiration from the exceptional 2e−ORR selectivity and potential for H2O2 production exhibited by the ECDs-CoSA catalyst, we employed a gas diffusion electrode (GDE) in conjunction with a flow-cell electrolyzer to facilitate practical synthesis of H2O2 in 1.0 M Na2SO4 solution (Fig. 6 a). As depicted in Fig. 6b, the LSV curve of the ECDs-CoSA catalyst demonstrated significantly higher current densities compared to ECDs, ECDs-CoSA0.1 and ECDs-CoSA0.5, strongly implyng the pivotal role played by Co sites within ECDs-CoSA in facilitating the electrosynthesis of H2O2. Bulk H2O2 production experiments were performed across a range of current densities from 0 to 0.4 V vs RHE (Fig. S16), and the cumulative concentration of H2O2 in the cathode electrolyte was determined using colorimetric quantification, enabling assessment of the rate of H2O2 generation (Fig. S17) [36]. As shown in Fig. 6c, the H2O2 yield exhibited a gradual increase with a negative shift in the operating potential, reaching a maximum of 3.1 mmol/L at 0 V vs RHE. Notably, the Faraday efficiency (FE) of H2O2 generation by ECDs-CoSA consistently around ∼80 % over the range of applied current densities. This can be attributed to the occurrence of self-decomposition or H2O2 reduction reaction when there is an increase in the concentration of H2O2 within the electrolyte [22]. To assess the durability of ECDs-CoSA, we performed a long-term electrolysis at 0.1 V vs RHE. As shown in Fig. 6d, ECDs-CoSA maintained a sustained H2O2 production efficiency (9.71 mol L−1 h−1 gcat. −1 and 83.27 % FE) for at least 60 h. On the contrary, ECDs-CoSAx with lower Co atoms loading exhibited unsatisfactory H2O2 yields and FE (Fig. 6e and Table S6), which further supports the positive impact of high Co atoms loading on H2O2 production. As illustrated in Fig. 6f and Table S1, the higher Co atoms loading of ECDs-CoSA prepared in this work significantly improved the H2O2 yield rate and FE, surpassing the most reported catalysts. Moreover, following the stability experiment, ECDs-CoSA maintained original morphology and structure intact (Fig. S18), underscoring its exceptional structural stability.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a Co-SACs with a high metal loading up to 6.16 wt% by utilizing CDs as carriers through a defect engineering strategy. Sufficient defects in ECDs provide numerous anchoring sites for Co atoms, effectively preventing their aggregation and promoting the formation of densely distributed single-atoms. Notably, with the increase of Co atoms loading, the ECDs-CoSA catalyst exhibited remarkable 2e−ORR selectivity up to 93.70 % in neutral conditions. According to the DFT calculation results, it was demonstrated that the Co-N3O2 active sites on ECDs-CoSA exhibited favorable O2 adsorption and facilitated the subsequent hydrogenation reaction of *OOH, ultimately leading to an enhancement of the 2e−ORR selectivity. In addition, ECDs-CoSA exhibited efficient H2O2 production achieved in a flow-cell electrolyzer at an impressive rate of 9.71 mol L−1 h−1 gcat. −1, surpassing the performance of most reported Co-SACs. This study offered a promising approach for developing Co-SACs with high Co atoms loading to address the deficiency of active sites for single atom catalysts in 2e−ORR. Based on this study, the approach of utilizing defect engineering to anchor single metal atoms onto CDs is anticipated to have broader applicability across various metals. Furthermore, it is feasible to modulate the catalytic reaction pathways via diverse metal centers, which in turn facilitates a deeper understanding of the underlying reaction mechanisms, ultimately leading to enhancements in catalytic activity and selectivity.
